**Eastertide** (also known as **Eastertime** or the **Easter season**) or **Paschaltide** (also known as **Paschaltime** or the **Paschal season**) is a festal season in the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") that focuses on celebrating the [Resurrection of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus_Christ "Resurrection of Jesus Christ"). Preceded by [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), it begins on [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") Day, which initiates [Easter Week](/wiki/Easter_Week "Easter Week") in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), and [Bright Week](/wiki/Bright_Week "Bright Week") in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity").

There are several [Eastertide customs](/wiki/Eastertide_customs "Eastertide customs") across the [Christian world](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom"), including [flowering the cross](/wiki/Flowering_the_cross "Flowering the cross"),[[1]](#cite_note-FGMC2021-1) [sunrise services](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service"), the wearing of [Easter bonnets](/wiki/Easter_bonnet "Easter bonnet") by women,[[2]](#cite_note-Friedman2016-2) exclaiming the [Paschal greeting](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting"), [clipping the church](/wiki/Clipping_the_church "Clipping the church"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and decorating [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_eggs "Easter eggs"), a symbol of the [empty tomb](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb").[[4]](#cite_note-Jordan2000-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Harbaugh1878-5)[[6]](#cite_note-GeddesGriffiths2002-6) Additional Eastertide traditions include [egg hunting](/wiki/Egg_hunting "Egg hunting"), eating special [Easter foods](/wiki/Easter_food "Easter food") and watching [Easter parades](/wiki/Easter_parade "Easter parade").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Davis-8) The [Easter lily](/wiki/Lilium_longiflorum "Lilium longiflorum"), a symbol of the resurrection in Christianity,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) traditionally decorates the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") area of [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") on this day and for the rest of Eastertide.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Traditionally lasting 40 days to commemorate the time the resurrected Jesus remained on earth before [his Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus"), in some western churches, Eastertide lasts 50 days to conclude on the day of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") or [Whitsunday](/wiki/Whitsunday "Whitsunday").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Western Christianity")]

Easter time is the period of 50 days, spanning from [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") Sunday to [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") Sunday.[[13]](#cite_note-13) It is celebrated as a single joyful feast, called the "great Lord's Day".[[14]](#cite_note-14) Each Sunday of the season is treated as a Sunday of Easter. In some traditions, Easter Sunday is the first Sunday of Eastertide and the following Sunday (Low Sunday) is the second Sunday of Eastertide and so on.[[15]](#cite_note-SWA2013-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Easter Sunday and Pentecost correspond to pre-existing Jewish feasts: The first day of [Pesach](/wiki/Pesach "Pesach") (פסח) and the holiday of [Shavu'ot](/wiki/Shavu%27ot "Shavu'ot") (שבועות). In the Jewish tradition, the 49 days between these holidays are known as [Counting of the Omer](/wiki/Counting_of_the_Omer "Counting of the Omer") (ספירת העומר)‎.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The first eight days constitute the [Octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)") of Easter and are celebrated as [solemnities](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity") of the Lord.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Since 2000, the Second Sunday of Easter is also called [Divine Mercy Sunday](/wiki/Divine_Mercy_Sunday "Divine Mercy Sunday"). The name "Low Sunday" for this Sunday, once common in English, is now used mainly in the Church of England.

The solemnity of the [Ascension of the Lord](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") is celebrated on the 40th day of Eastertide (a Thursday), except in countries where it is not a [Holy Day of Obligation](/wiki/Holy_Day_of_Obligation "Holy Day of Obligation"). In such countries it is celebrated on the following Sunday (the 43rd day of Eastertide).[[19]](#cite_note-19) The nine days from that feast until the Saturday before Pentecost (inclusive) are days of preparation for the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit "Holy Spirit") the Paraclete,[[20]](#cite_note-20) which inspired the form of prayer called a [novena](/wiki/Novena "Novena").

Before the 1969 revision of the calendar, the Sundays were called First Sunday *after* Easter, Second Sunday *after* Easter, etc. The Sunday preceding the feast of the Ascension of the Lord was sometimes, although not officially, called [Rogation Sunday](/wiki/Rogation_days "Rogation days"), and when the Ascension had an octave, the following Sunday was called Sunday within the Octave of the Ascension.[[21]](#cite_note-21) When this octave was abolished in 1955, it was called Sunday after the Ascension.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Pentecost was followed by an octave, which some reckoned as part of Eastertide.

When the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") and Lutheran churches implemented their own calendar and lectionary reforms in 1976, they adopted the same shortened definition of the Easter season as the Roman Catholic Church had promulgated six years earlier. In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the Easter season begins with the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") and ends after [Evening Prayer](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)") (or [Night Prayer](/wiki/Compline "Compline")) on the Day of Pentecost. Some Anglican [provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") continue to label the Sundays between Easter and the Ascension "Sundays After Easter" rather than "Sundays of Easter"; others, such as the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and [ECUSA](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Episcopal Church in the United States of America"), use the term "Sundays of Easter". Anglican provinces and dioceses which continue to use The Book of Common Prayer (1662) as their standard of doctrine and liturgy persist in having a traditional 40 day Eastertide which concludes on Ascension Day, also known as Holy Thursday.

The [Te Deum](/wiki/Te_Deum "Te Deum") and Gloria are recited every day even in the ferial Office. On Sundays the "Asperges" is replaced by the "Vidi Aquam" which recalls the solemn baptism of Easter eve. There is no feast day from Easter until Ascension. The Armenians during this period do away even with the abstinence on Fridays. Prayers are said standing, not kneeling. Instead of the "Angelus" the "Regina Caeli" is recited. From Easter to Ascension many churches, about the tenth century, said only one Nocturn at Matins; even some particular churches in the city of Rome adopted this custom from the Teutons (Bäumer, "Gesch. des Breviers", 312).

[Pope Gregory VII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_VII "Pope Gregory VII") limited this privilege to the week of Easter and of Pentecost. Some dioceses in Germany retained it far into the nineteenth century for 40 days after Easter. In every [Nocturn](/wiki/Nocturn "Nocturn") the three psalms are said under one [antiphon](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon"). The [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") appears as an independent antiphon. An Alleluia is added to all the antiphons, responsories and versicles, except to the versicles of the preces at [Prime](/wiki/Prime_(liturgy) "Prime (liturgy)") and [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline"). Instead of the "suffragia sanctorum" in the semidouble and ferial Offices, a commemoration of the Holy Cross is used. The iambic hymns have a special Easter [doxology](/wiki/Doxology "Doxology").

The feasts of the holy Apostles and martyrs have their own commune from Easter to Pentecost. At Mass the Alleluia is added to the Introit, Offertory and Communion; in place of the Gradual two Alleluias are sung followed by two verses, each with an Alleluia; there is also a special Preface for Paschal Time.

Paschal Tide was the period during which every member of the faithful who has attained the year of discretion was bound by the positive law of the Church to receive Holy Communion (Easter duty). During the early Middle Ages from the time of the Synod of Agde (508), it was customary to receive Holy Communion at least three times a year—Christmas, Easter and Pentecost. A positive precept was issued by the [Fourth Lateran Council](/wiki/Fourth_Lateran_Council "Fourth Lateran Council") (1215) and confirmed by the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") (Sess. XIII, can. ix). According to these decrees the faithful of either sex, after coming to the age of discretion, must receive at least at Easter the Sacrament of the Eucharist (unless by the advice of the parish priest they abstain for a while). Otherwise during life they are to be prevented from entering the church and when dead are to be denied Christian burial. The paschal precept is to be fulfilled in one's parish church.

Although the precept of the Fourth Lateran to confess to the parish priest fell into disuse and permission was given to confess anywhere, the precept of receiving Easter Communion in the parish church was still in force where there are canonically erected parishes.

The term Paschal Tide was usually interpreted to mean the two weeks between Palm and Low Sundays (Synod of Avignon, 1337). By [St. Antonine of Florence](/wiki/St._Antonine_of_Florence "St. Antonine of Florence") it was restricted to Easter Sunday, Monday and Tuesday. By Angelo da Chiavasso it was defined as the period from [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") to Low Sunday. [Eugene IV](/wiki/Eugene_IV "Eugene IV"), 8 July 1440, authoritatively interpreted it to mean the two weeks between Palm and Low Sundays.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In later centuries the time has been variously extended: at Naples from [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") to Ascension; at Palermo from [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") to Low Sunday. In Germany, at an early date, the second Sunday after Easter terminated Paschal Tide, for which reason it was called "Predigerkirchweih", because the hard Easter labour was over, or "Buch Sunday", the obstinate sinners putting off the fulfillment of the precept to the last day. In the United States upon petition of the Fathers of the [First Provincial Council of Baltimore](/wiki/First_Provincial_Council_of_Baltimore "First Provincial Council of Baltimore") Paschal Tide was extended by [Pius VIII](/wiki/Pius_VIII "Pius VIII") to the period from the first Sunday in Lent to Trinity Sunday (II Plen. Coun. Balt., n. 257); in Canada the duration of the Paschal Tide is the same as in the United States. In England it lasts from Ash Wednesday until Low Sunday; in Ireland from Ash Wednesday until the octave of SS. Peter and Paul, 6 July.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Liturgical aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Liturgical aspects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Our_Savior_Lutheran_Church_Baltimore_Easter_Altar.jpg/330px-Our_Savior_Lutheran_Church_Baltimore_Easter_Altar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Our_Savior_Lutheran_Church_Baltimore_Easter_Altar.jpg)

[Easter lilies](/wiki/Lilium_longiflorum "Lilium longiflorum"), a symbol of the resurrection of Jesus, adorn the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") of a Lutheran church in Baltimore

Paschal Tide is a season of joy. The liturgical color is white (and sometimes gold), which is the color designated for feasts and festivals. The [Paschal candle](/wiki/Paschal_candle "Paschal candle") is lit on Easter and on Sundays during Eastertide. Eastertide ends on the Day of Pentecost.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The [Easter lily](/wiki/Lilium_longiflorum "Lilium longiflorum"), a symbol of the resurrection,[[26]](#cite_note-Collins2014-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) traditionally serve as the [chancel flowers](/wiki/Chancel_flowers "Chancel flowers") that decorate the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") area of [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") throughout Eastertide.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The Orthodox (Greek) Church celebrates on Friday of Easter Week the feast of Our Lady, the Living Fountain (shrine at Constantinople).[[29]](#cite_note-Holweck-29)

The Sundays from Easter to [Ascension Day](/wiki/Ascension_Day "Ascension Day"), besides being called the First, Second (etc.) Sunday in Easteride or after Easter Sunday, have their own peculiar titles.[[15]](#cite_note-SWA2013-15)

#### First Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First Sunday")]

Main article: [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday")

#### Second Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Second Sunday")]

Main article: [Second Sunday of Easter](/wiki/Second_Sunday_of_Easter "Second Sunday of Easter")

The Second Sunday of Easter is also known in Western Christianity as [Divine Mercy Sunday](/wiki/Divine_Mercy_Sunday "Divine Mercy Sunday"), the Octave Day of Easter, White Sunday, Quasimodo Sunday, and Low Sunday. In Eastern Christianity, this day is known as Antipascha, New Sunday (or Renewal Sunday), and Thomas Sunday.

#### Third Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Third Sunday")]

Main article: [Third Sunday of Easter](/wiki/Third_Sunday_of_Easter "Third Sunday of Easter")

Traditionally, the Third Sunday of Easter was also called Misericordia Sunday and Good Shepherd Sunday in Western Christianity. Subsequent to modern liturgical reforms, these names are often applied to the Fourth Sunday instead. In Greek Orthodoxy, this day is called the Sunday of the Myrrhbearers.

#### Fourth Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Fourth Sunday")]

Main article: [Fourth Sunday of Easter](/wiki/Fourth_Sunday_of_Easter "Fourth Sunday of Easter")

Traditionally, the Fourth Sunday of Easter was also called Jubilate Sunday in Western Christianity. Subsequent to modern liturgical reforms, this name is often more appropriately applied to the Third Sunday instead. The Fourth Sunday of Easter is also known as Good Shepherd Sunday and Vocations Sunday. In Greek Orthodoxy, this day is called the Sunday of the Paralytic.

#### Fifth Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fifth Sunday")]

Main article: [Cantate Sunday](/wiki/Cantate_Sunday "Cantate Sunday")

The Asian Churches on Wednesday after the third Sunday celebrate with a very solemn Office and an octave, the Mesopentekoste, the completion of the first half of Paschal Tide; it is the feast of the manifestation of the Messiah; the Slav nations in this day have a solemn procession and benediction of their rivers.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
The fifth Sunday is called [Cantate Sunday](/wiki/Cantate_Sunday "Cantate Sunday");[[31]](#cite_note-31) by the Orientals it is called Sunday of the Samaritan Woman.

#### Sixth Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sixth Sunday")]

The sixth Sunday, "Vocem jucunditatis" in Asia, Sunday of the Man Born Blind. In the Latin Church follow the [Rogation Days](/wiki/Rogation_Days "Rogation Days"); in the Greek Church on Tuesday is kept the apodosis or conclusion of the feast of Easter. The Greeks sing the Canons of Easter up to this Tuesday in the same manner as during Easter Week, whilst in the Latin Church the specific Easter Office terminates on Saturday following the feast. Thursday is the feast of the Ascension. The Friday of this week, in Germany, is called "Witterfreitag"; the fields are blessed against frost and thunderstorms.

#### Seventh Sunday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Seventh Sunday")]

The Sunday that falls within the octave of Ascension is called "Exaudi" from the Introit; in some dioceses it is called Feast of Our Lady, Queen of the Apostles (double major) or of the Cenacle (first class); in Rome it was called [Sunday of the Roses](/wiki/Rosalia_(festival)#Rose_Sundays "Rosalia (festival)") ("Pascha rosarum" or "rosatum"), since in the Pantheon rose-leaves were thrown from the rotunda into the church; in the Greek and Russian Churches it is the feast of the 318 Fathers of the first Nicene Council; the Armenians call it the "second feast of the flowers", a repetition of Palm Sunday.

By older liturgists the week before Pentecost is called "Hebdomada expectationis", week of the expectation of the Holy Ghost. On the Vigil of Pentecost the baptismal water is blessed in the Latin Church. In the Oriental Churches, that Saturday is the *psychosabbaton* (All Soul's Day), upon which the Greeks bless wheat cakes and hold processions to the cemeteries.

## Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

### Byzantine Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Byzantine Rite")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Crucession_Davidovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8526.jpg/500px-Crucession_Davidovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8526.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crucession_Davidovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8526.jpg)

[Bright Week](/wiki/Bright_Week "Bright Week") procession ([Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Old-Rite_Church "Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church") in [Guslitsa](/wiki/Guslitsa "Guslitsa"))

Main article: [Pentecostarion](/wiki/Pentecostarion "Pentecostarion")

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") begins on Easter Sunday at [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") which is normally celebrated at midnight and continues for forty days through the ninth hour on the day before the [Ascension](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension").

### East Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastertide&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: East Syriac Rite")]

The [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") refers to Eastertide as the *Season of Resurrection*, also known by the Syriac [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") *Qyamta* and the season runs up to the feast of Pentecost.

In this rite, the following feasts are fixed to various days of the Season of Resurrection:[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

* Friday after Easter: [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day")
* Fourth Sunday after Easter ("Fifth Sunday of Resurrection"): [Mar Addai](/wiki/Thaddeus_of_Edessa "Thaddeus of Edessa")
* Sixth Thursday after Easter: [Ascension of Our Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension")

The [Second Sunday of Resurrection](/wiki/Second_Sunday_of_Easter "Second Sunday of Easter") is also known as "New Sunday" or "St. Thomas Sunday".
